ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific concept for Chicken Tikka. Nearest parent is a marinated grilled chicken product. A new concept for Indian tandoor-cooked chicken preparations is recommended.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No exact FoodEx2 code for Chicken Tikka; A11KM used as closest parent for marinated grilled poultry.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept identified for Chicken Tikka as a prepared food item.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard individual serving of Chicken Tikka is approximately 200 g, equivalent to 4–6 boneless pieces as typically served in an Indian restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken Tikka — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods, Entry F11020024; cross-referenced with HPB Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research — National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN); Health Promotion Board (HPB), Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced from ICMR-NIN entry F11020024 for Chicken Tikka, cross-referenced with HPB Singapore Foodo for secondary validation. Core macros and micronutrients confirmed at confidence 1.0. Copper, manganese, and vitamin K values sourced from enriched JSON at confidence 0.9, consistent with USDA FoodData Central reference values for marinated grilled chicken.

gi_source.title: Estimated GI for Chicken Tikka based on analogous high-protein, low-carbohydrate grilled chicken preparation; reference: University of Sydney GI Database — grilled chicken and high-protein low-carbohydrate foods
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for Chicken Tikka in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 15 based on analogous high-protein, very-low-carbohydrate grilled chicken preparations. Carbohydrate content is 3.5 g/100g, primarily from yogurt marinade and spices. Glycemic load per 200 g serving = (15 × 7.0) / 100 = 1.05, rounded to 1.1. This is consistent with the negligible glycemic impact expected from a protein-dominant, low-carbohydrate food.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content 425 mg/100g; a 200 g serving delivers approximately 850 mg sodium
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 100–150 g and request reduced-salt marinade preparations, as a standard 200 g restaurant serving provides approximately 850 mg sodium, representing 37–43% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000–2300 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content (3.5 g/100g) and high protein (22.5 g/100g) with estimated GI of 15
type2_diabetes.guidance: Chicken Tikka is well-suited for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its very low carbohydrate content (3.5 g/100g), high protein (22.5 g/100g), and estimated glycemic index of 15, resulting in a glycemic load of approximately 1.1 per 200 g serving, which has negligible impact on postprandial blood glucose.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate total fat (10.5 g/100g) with relatively low saturated fat (2.8 g/100g); elevated sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Chicken Tikka provides a lean protein source with moderate fat (10.5 g/100g) and low saturated fat (2.8 g/100g), which is broadly compatible with a heart-healthy diet; however, the elevated sodium content (425 mg/100g) warrants portion control and preference for home-prepared or reduced-salt versions for individuals with cardiovascular disease.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High selenium (28 mcg/100g) and anti-inflammatory spices (turmeric/curcumin)
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Chicken Tikka is a good dietary choice for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis, providing 28 mcg selenium per 100g (a key antioxidant supporting immune function) and curcumin from turmeric in the spice blend, which has documented anti-inflammatory properties.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chicken Tikka is one of the most iconic North Indian and Punjabi starter dishes, widely popular across South Asia, the United Kingdom, and Southeast Asia. It consists of boneless pieces of chicken — typically breast or thigh — marinated for several hours in a spiced yogurt mixture containing ginger-garlic paste, Kashmiri red chilli powder (deghi mirch), cumin, coriander, garam masala, turmeric, and lemon juice. The marinated chicken is skewered and cooked at high heat in a traditional tandoor clay oven, producing a characteristic smoky aroma, charred edges, tender and juicy interior, and a vibrant orange-red colour from the chilli and turmeric. It is served dry (without sauce) as an appetiser or starter, typically garnished with sliced raw onion rings, lemon wedges, and accompanied by mint-coriander chutney. Nutritionally, Chicken Tikka is high in protein (22.5 g/100g), moderate in fat (10.5 g/100g), very low in carbohydrates (3.5 g/100g), and rich in selenium (28 mcg/100g), niacin (8.5 mg/100g), and vitamin B6 (0.52 mg/100g). Its very low glycemic index (estimated 15) and negligible glycemic load make it suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. Sodium content is elevated at 425 mg/100g, requiring portion awareness for those with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. It is halal and gluten-free in its traditional preparation.
common_user_queries: chicken tikka vs chicken tikka masala

confidence_reason: Core macros and the majority of micronutrients sourced from ICMR-NIN entry F11020024 (exact match) at confidence 1.0, cross-referenced with HPB Singapore Foodo. Three minor micronutrients (copper, manganese, vitamin K) sourced from enriched JSON at confidence 0.9, consistent with USDA FoodData Central reference ranges for marinated grilled chicken. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database framework, reducing overall confidence to high rather than definitive.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for Chicken Tikka not available in ICMR-NIN entry F11020024 or HPB Singapore Foodo database as of 2026-04-13. water_g set to -1: moisture content not reported in the primary ICMR-NIN source for this preparation. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero per ICMR-NIN source). Copper (0.08 mg/100g), manganese (0.04 mg/100g), and vitamin K (0.5 mcg/100g) sourced from enriched JSON at confidence 0.9; consistent with USDA FoodData Central values for comparable grilled chicken preparations. GI of 15 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Chicken Tikka exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per 200 g serving calculated as (15 × 7.0 g carbohydrate) / 100 = 1.05, reported as 1.1. Portions array from input contained multiple duplicates and non-standard entries (wraps, piadina, set meals); the canonical serving size of 200 g (4–6 pieces) is used as the primary serving reference, consistent with ICMR-NIN and verified source entries. Hypertension suitability note: the input record marked hypertension as suitable=true but noted elevated sodium (~850 mg per 200 g serving); health context has been set to concern_level moderate to reflect this accurately.
